icd_category: N04.0 — Nephrotic syndrome with minor glomerular abnormality
one_liner: A 6-year-old boy with no significant past medical history presents with facial puffiness and bilateral leg swelling noticed by his parents over the past week.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient's mother reports progressive swelling around his eyes each morning for the past 7 days, with bilateral leg and scrotal swelling developing over the past 3 days. She noticed his urine has been 'foamy.' He had a mild upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago that resolved. He has had decreased appetite and mild abdominal discomfort. No hematuria, fever, rash, or joint pain. No recent medication changes.
stage2.pmh: Full-term birth, normal development. No prior hospitalizations. Up to date on vaccinations.
stage2.family_history: No family history of kidney disease. Mother has seasonal allergies. Father is healthy.
stage2.social_history: Attends first grade. Lives with both parents and an older sister. No recent travel. Has a pet cat at home.
stage2.physical_exam: Well-appearing but edematous child in no acute distress. Periorbital edema is prominent bilaterally. No rash or petechiae. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: **decreased breath sounds at right base with dullness to percussion**. Abdomen: **mildly distended, shifting dullness present, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly**. Extremities: **3+ pitting edema of bilateral lower extremities to the knees**; **Genitalia: moderate scrotal edema**. Skin: **shiny and taut over edematous areas**.

lab_results.result: 3+ protein, oval fat bodies, no RBCs, no WBCs, fatty casts
lab_results.clinical_significance: Heavy proteinuria with lipiduria characteristic of this condition
Severe hypoalbuminemia drives edema formation in this condition
Hyperlipidemia is a hallmark of this condition due to hepatic lipoprotein overproduction
Elevated as part of nephrotic hyperlipidemia
Normal renal function; minimal change disease typically preserves GFR
Pseudohyponatremia from hyperlipidemia or dilutional from fluid retention
Normal complement levels help exclude membranoproliferative GN, lupus nephritis, and post-infectious GN
Confirms nephrotic-range proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr in children)

imaging_results.result: Kidneys are normal in size and echogenicity bilaterally. No hydronephrosis, masses, or renal vein thrombosis.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Excludes structural abnormalities; normal appearance is typical in minimal change disease

imaging_results.result: Small right-sided pleural effusion. No consolidation or cardiomegaly.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Pleural effusion secondary to low oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminemia

stage5.final_clue: A young child presenting with nephrotic-range proteinuria (UPC 12.5), severe hypoalbuminemia (1.4 g/dL), hyperlipidemia, and anasarca following a recent URI—with normal complement levels and preserved renal function—is classic for this condition, the most common cause of this condition in children.

debrief.classic_presentation: A child aged 2-8 years presenting with periorbital edema (worst in the morning), anasarca, foamy urine, and weight gain, often following a viral URI. Labs reveal nephrotic-range proteinuria, severe hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and normal complement levels.
debrief.pathophysiology: Minimal change disease (MCD) involves T-cell-mediated injury to podocyte foot processes, leading to effacement of foot processes visible only on electron microscopy. Light microscopy and immunofluorescence are essentially normal. The damaged glomerular filtration barrier allows massive albumin loss into the urine. Hypoalbuminemia decreases plasma oncotic pressure, promoting fluid shifts into the interstitial space (edema). The liver compensates by increasing protein and lipoprotein synthesis, causing hyperlipidemia.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line therapy is oral prednisone (2 mg/kg/day or 60 mg/m²/day, max 60 mg) for 4-6 weeks, followed by a taper over 2-3 months. Approximately 80-90% of children with MCD are steroid-responsive. Supportive care includes salt restriction, judicious diuretic use for severe edema, and albumin infusion for symptomatic hypovolemia. Steroid-dependent or frequently relapsing patients may require steroid-sparing agents (cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors, mycophenolate, or rituximab). Renal biopsy is indicated only for steroid-resistant cases.
high_yield_pearls: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (approximately 80% of cases in ages 1-10).
Normal C3/C4 complement levels help distinguish MCD from post-infectious GN, membranoproliferative GN, and lupus nephritis.
Light microscopy is normal in MCD; diagnosis requires electron microscopy showing diffuse podocyte foot process effacement.

Children with first-episode nephrotic syndrome aged 1-12 are treated empirically with steroids WITHOUT biopsy. Biopsy is reserved for steroid-resistant disease, atypical features (hematuria, low complement, hypertension, renal failure), or age <1 or >12.
Patients with nephrotic syndrome are at increased risk for thromboembolism (due to loss of antithrombin III) and infection (due to loss of immunoglobulins and complement factors).
sources: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Glomerulonephritis (2021)
UpToDate: Minimal change disease — etiology, clinical features, and diagnosis (2024)
Eddy AA, Symons JM. Nephrotic syndrome in childhood. Lancet. 2003;362(9384):629-639.

after_stage2_more_likely: Minimal change disease — young child with classic nephrotic presentation (edema, foamy urine) following viral URI
FSGS — similar presentation, but less common in this age group
after_stage2_less_likely: Post-infectious GN — typically presents with nephritic syndrome (hematuria, hypertension), not pure nephrotic syndrome
IgA nephropathy — usually presents with hematuria, not isolated proteinuria and edema
after_labs_top3: Minimal change disease — nephrotic-range proteinuria, severe hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, normal complement, preserved renal function in a young child
FSGS — can present identically on labs; distinguished only by biopsy (would show segmental sclerosis)
Membranous nephropathy — very rare in children; normal complement makes this less likely
